stories.title: Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bear Recall
stories.angle: CPSC recalled the Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bear (model 034464) — a weighted plush bear with a side pouch containing 2.5 lbs of ceramic beads. The zipper slider can detach, posing a choking hazard to children. Approximately 36,000 units were sold in the US from January through March 2026 at approximately $48 in Build-A-Bear stores and at buildabear.com. Consumers should stop using the bear immediately and return it to any Build-A-Bear Workshop store for a refund (original payment or gift card). Recall announced April 30, 2026. Contact: 844-541-0144 or buildabear.com/recalls.

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
RECALL: Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bears — zipper slider detaches, choking hazard to kids. ⚠️

~36,000 sold Jan–Mar 2026 (~$48). Return to any Build-A-Bear store for a refund (original payment or gift card).

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Bought a Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bear this year? Return it now.

CPSC confirmed: the zipper slider can detach — choking hazard. Call 844-541-0144 or bring it to any Build-A-Bear store for a refund. ⚠️

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
If you or your kids received a Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bear this year, you'll want to stop using it and return it now.

The CPSC has officially recalled the Heartwarming Hugs Bear (model 034464) — a weighted plush bear with a side pouch holding about 2.5 pounds of ceramic beads that can be heated or cooled for comfort. The problem: the zipper slider on that side pouch can detach. If a young child gets hold of it, it's a choking hazard. The agency is treating this as a risk of serious injury or death.

About 36,000 of these bears were sold in the US from January through March 2026 at Build-A-Bear Workshop stores and on buildabear.com — at around $48. The recall was announced April 30, 2026.

The fix is simple: stop using the bear and return it to any Build-A-Bear Workshop store. The company will refund you in your original form of payment or with a gift card for the purchase price, redeemable on any merchandise. You can also call Build-A-Bear at 844-541-0144 (Mon–Sat 8 a.m.–5 p.m. CT; Sun 7 a.m.–4:30 p.m. CT) or visit buildabear.com/recalls for details.

Do your kids have one of these? Let other COS families know in the comments. ⚠️
facebook.image_caption: RECALL: Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bears. The zipper slider can detach — choking hazard. Return to any Build-A-Bear store for a full refund. ~36,000 sold Jan–Mar 2026. ⚠️

article.headline: Build-A-Bear Heartwarming Hugs Bear Recalled Over Choking Hazard

stories.title: AAP Drops Strict Screen Time Limits — New 2026 Guidelines
stories.angle: The American Academy of Pediatrics officially dropped its previous 1-hour-per-day screen time limit model in January 2026 and moved to a new quality-and-context framework called the "5 Cs" — Child (age, temperament), Content (what they're watching), Calm (whether screens overstimulate), Crowding out (whether screens are displacing sleep/movement/family time), and Communication (talking about digital habits). No specific daily hour limit is now recommended for children over 5. Under-18-month guidance (no screens except video calls) and device-free bedrooms/mealtimes remain.

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Big change: the AAP no longer recommends strict daily screen time limits for most kids. 📱

New focus is on what screens displace — sleep, movement, family time. Plus who they're watching with and what they're watching.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
The AAP's new screen time framework: the "5 Cs."

Child (age, temperament), Content, Calm (overstimulation?), Crowding out (replacing sleep/play?), Communication.

Less about the clock. More about context.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
If you've been counting screen minutes and feeling guilty about it, the American Academy of Pediatrics just gave you permission to stop clock-watching.

In January 2026, the AAP released updated digital media guidelines that officially moved away from the old strict daily time limits — like "no more than one hour per day" — and shifted toward a new framework. Their reasoning: what kids are watching matters more than how long, and what screens are displacing (like sleep or family time) matters more than any raw number.

The new framework is built around five questions, which the AAP calls the "5 Cs":
• Child — What's this child's age, temperament, and current needs?
• Content — Is what they're watching or playing high-quality and age-appropriate?
• Calm — Does screen time help them relax, or is it overstimulating?
• Crowding out — Is it replacing sleep, physical activity, or real-world connection?
• Communication — Are you talking about what they're doing online?

A few things didn't change: under-18-month-olds should still have no screens except video calls. Device-free bedrooms and mealtimes are still recommended. And turning screens off at least an hour before bed remains solid guidance.

The shift is meaningful. Instead of a guilt-producing number, you're asking whether screen time is fitting into your family's life well — and if it's getting in the way of the things that matter most.

What's been your approach to screen time in your family? 📱
facebook.image_caption: The AAP dropped strict daily screen time limits in 2026 — new focus is on the "5 Cs": Child, Content, Calm, Crowding out, and Communication. Less clock-watching, more context. What's your take? 📱

article.headline: The AAP Just Changed Its Screen Time Guidance. Here's What's Actually Different.

stories.title: D11 Free Summer Lunch — Still Running Through July 31
stories.angle: D11's Summer Food Service Program is still running through approximately July 31, 2026. Any child or teen ages 1-18 can receive a free lunch — no registration needed. One key COS location: East Library mobile unit at 5550 N. Union Blvd., serving 11 a.m.–noon Monday–Friday. Multiple sites throughout the city. Contact D11 Food and Nutrition Services at 719-520-2930 or find all locations at d11.org/summermeals.

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Free lunch for kids under 18 this week in Colorado Springs. 🥪

D11 Summer Food Service runs through July 31. East Library mobile unit (5550 N. Union Blvd.) serves 11 a.m.–noon weekdays. No signup needed.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Quick reminder for COS families: free lunch is still available for kids and teens through the end of the month.

D11's Summer Food Service Program runs through approximately July 31, and any child or teen ages 1-18 can eat for free. No registration, no paperwork — just show up during service hours. If you're in northeast Colorado Springs, the East Library location is especially convenient: a D11 mobile unit serves free lunch from 11 a.m. to noon, Monday through Friday, at 5550 N. Union Blvd.

There are locations across the city, including mobile home parks, community centers, and school sites like Grant, Jackson, and Keller elementaries. You can find the full list of sites and hours at d11.org/summermeals or call D11 Food and Nutrition Services at 719-520-2930.

Adult meals are available at some sites for $5. The program closes at the end of July, so there are about two weeks left to use it.

Which D11 summer lunch site is closest to you? 🥪
facebook.image_caption: Free lunch for any child ages 1-18 through July 31 — D11 Summer Food Service. East Library mobile unit at 5550 N. Union Blvd. runs 11 a.m.–noon weekdays. No signup needed. Full site list at d11.org/summermeals. 🥪

stories.title: COS Family Events This Week — July 21-27
stories.angle: Good low-cost and free options this week: Kids Dinosaur Yoga at Ruth Holley Library (Monday July 21, 10:30 a.m., free); Bear Creek Nature Center Bumblebee Program with naturalist Susan Crick (Friday July 25, 10–11:30 a.m., $5, pre-registration required); Zinnia Days at Gather Mountain Blooms — cut-your-own zinnia field (Friday July 25, 8 a.m.–noon, $10 general admission, $25 Cup of Flowers upgrade; on-site coffee, brunch, market); Sheet Lighting Night at Fountain Creek Nature Center with Mile High Bug Club (Friday July 25, 8:30 p.m.–midnight, free, RSVP required at 719-520-6745).

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
COS outdoor picks for Fri Jul 25 🌻

Bumblebees at Bear Creek — 10 a.m., $5, pre-register
Night bugs at Fountain Creek — 8:30 p.m., free, RSVP
Zinnia pick at Gather Mtn Blooms — 8 a.m., $10

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
A few things worth getting outside for this week in Colorado Springs, especially if you've got kids who need a change of scenery.

On Monday, July 21, Ruth Holley Library is running Kids Dinosaur Yoga at 10:30 a.m. — free, and a nice start to the week for the younger set.

If you're looking to make Friday special, July 25 has three distinct options:

The Bear Creek Nature Center is hosting a Bumblebee Program from 10 to 11:30 a.m. Naturalist Susan Crick will lead the trail, covering how to ID bumblebees, why they're critical for wildflowers and vegetables, how to create habitat, and why populations are declining. It's $5 per person and requires pre-registration.

Gather Mountain Blooms opens its zinnia field for a pick-your-own event from 8 a.m. to noon. General admission is $10 (children under 6 free); the $25 Cup of Flowers upgrade includes snips and up to 20 stems to take home. On-site: Good Gracious Coffee, 4th Avenue Crepes, a mini makers market, and floral cocktails for the adults. It's at 5210 South U.S. Highway 85-87. Book ahead at gathermountainblooms.com.

Then for the night owls: the Mile High Bug Club is hosting a Sheet Lighting Night at Fountain Creek Nature Center starting at 8:30 p.m. (runs until midnight). A naturalist-guided watch for moths, beetles, and other nocturnal insects drawn to a light sheet. It's free but requires an RSVP — call 719-520-6745 or visit elpasocountynaturecenters.com.

What's your family doing this week? 🌻
facebook.image_caption: COS family picks for this week: Bumblebee trail at Bear Creek (Fri, $5), zinnia pick at Gather Mountain Blooms (Fri 8 a.m., $10), and a free night bug watch at Fountain Creek (Fri 8:30 p.m., RSVP). What's on your list? 🌻

stories.title: SDADI Kitchen Step Stool Recall — July 16, 2026
stories.angle: CPSC recalled SDADI kitchen helper tower step stools (models LT01 and LT05, importer: Yiwushi Bihe Trading) on July 16, 2026. The stools can collapse or tip over while in use, and a child's torso can fit through openings on the sides — posing entrapment, fall, and tip-over hazards. About 5,952 units were sold on Amazon, Walmart.com, Target.com, and BestBuy.com from April 2024 through September 2025 at $45–$155. 8 reports of instability or tip-overs; 4 resulting injuries. Remedy: full refund — stop use, store away from children, contact john@SDADI.net.

x_posts.label: Post 5-1
x_posts.text:
RECALL: SDADI kitchen step stools (LT01, LT05) — collapse, tip-over, and entrapment risk. ⚠️

~6,000 sold Apr 2024–Sep 2025 ($45–$155) at Amazon, Walmart, Target. Stop use and email john@SDADI.net for a refund.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 5-2
x_posts.text:
If you have a SDADI step stool — stop using it. It can collapse or tip over with a child inside. ⚠️

July 16 CPSC recall covers models LT01 and LT05. 4 injuries reported. Full refund available.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
If you bought a SDADI kitchen tower step stool in the last year or two, stop using it and move it away from your kids.

The CPSC issued a formal recall on July 16 — just four days ago — for SDADI kitchen helper tower stools, models LT01 and LT05, imported by Yiwushi Bihe Trading. The problem: the stools can collapse or tip over while being used, and the openings on the front and back sides are large enough for a child's torso to pass through. That's an entrapment, fall, and tip-over hazard — the CPSC rates the risk as potential serious injury or death.

About 5,952 of these were sold from April 2024 through September 2025 on Amazon, Walmart.com, Target.com, and BestBuy.com. They came in gray, white, espresso, pink, and natural wood finishes at prices ranging from $45 to $155. The CPSC received 8 reports of instability or tip-overs; 4 of those resulted in injuries including scrapes, cuts, and bruises.

To get a refund: stop using the stool, store it somewhere your kids can't get to it, and email john@SDADI.net. You'll receive a full refund for the purchase price.

Worth double-checking if you have step stools from Amazon in your home — this brand was widely sold and may not have come with much packaging that would make the brand name obvious.

Do you recognize this product? Let us know in the comments so other families can check too. ⚠️
facebook.image_caption: RECALL: SDADI kitchen tower step stools, models LT01 and LT05. Can collapse, tip over, and trap a child. Issued July 16, 2026. Stop use now and email john@SDADI.net for a full refund. ⚠️
